1:
"Then the LORD said unto Moses, Go in unto Pharaoh, and tell him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me." The witnesses note God sending Moses again to Pharaoh with the same demand. Gill notes this was the same day the plague of the flies was removed. Matthew Henry notes here is warning given of another plague, the murrain of beasts. The witnesses note God bidding Moses go in to Pharaoh and renew the demand in the name of the LORD God of the Hebrews, that he let his people go to serve him; the persistent, unchanging demand that opens each new plague — that God comes again with the same word: "Go in unto Pharaoh, and tell him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me"; the witnesses note the constancy of the demand, "Let my people go, that they may serve me" — the same just requirement pressed through plague after plague, that Israel be freed to worship their God; the witnesses note the authority, "Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews" — that Moses comes not in his own name but with the word of Jehovah, the covenant God; the witnesses note the warning before the blow, that God sends his message and demand before the murrain falls, giving Pharaoh yet another space to obey; the witnesses note the patience of God, who repeats his call to the stubborn, that they be left without excuse; so that God renews the demand before the fifth plague; Let my people go, that they may serve me. The witnesses note Go in unto Pharaoh, and tell him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews. The witnesses note Let my people go, that they may serve me.

The witnesses note God sending Moses again with the same unchanging demand — "Let my people go, that they may serve me" — pressed through plague after plague; the patience of God, who warns before the blow and repeats his call to the stubborn, that they be left without excuse.

So the witnesses make the verse the persistent demand that opens each plague. The lesson the expositors draw is that God renewed the same just demand in his own name before the murrain, the patience that warns before striking. The witnesses note the go in to Pharaoh: "Go in unto Pharaoh" — Gill notes the same day the flies ceased; the go in to Pharaoh; the the same day; the the flies; the removed; the again. The expositors observe the thus saith the LORD: "Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews" — the authority; the thus saith the LORD; the the authority; the the covenant God; the Jehovah; the the word. The witnesses press the let my people go: "Let my people go, that they may serve me" — the constant demand; the let my people go; the the constant demand; the to serve; the to worship; the freed. The expositors draw the lesson on the warning before: the warning before the blow; the warning before; the the warning; the the blow; the another space; the to obey. The witnesses observe the the patience: the patience of God; the the patience; the patience; the repeats; the the stubborn; the without excuse. The expositors note the Henry's note: Henry notes warning of the murrain; the Henry's note; the Henry; the warning; the the murrain; the of beasts. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Go in unto Pharaoh and tell him Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, the Let my people go that they may serve me, and the demand. The verse thus records the persistent demand — teaching that God renewed the same just demand before the murrain, the patience that warns before striking.
2:
"For if thou refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still," The witnesses note the condition of the plague: if Pharaoh still refuses and holds Israel. Keil and Delitzsch note the threat is conditioned, "if thou refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still," the plague following on continued refusal. The witnesses note the LORD setting the condition, "if thou refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still"; the just ground of the coming judgment, Pharaoh's continued and willful refusal — that God makes the plague to hang on Pharaoh's own obstinacy: "if thou refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still"; the witnesses note the conditional form, that the murrain is threatened "if" Pharaoh "refuse" — that the judgment was not arbitrary but the just answer to his persistent disobedience, in his own power to avert by obeying; the witnesses note the doubling, "refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still," marking both the negative (he would not release) and the positive (he would keep holding them in bondage), his settled and active resistance; the witnesses note that God deals fairly with Pharaoh, laying the blame and the choice plainly before him, so that the plague, when it came, should be seen to be of his own procuring; the witnesses note that sinners bring God's judgments on themselves by their own obstinate refusal; so that the plague hangs on Pharaoh's continued refusal; and wilt hold them still. The witnesses note For if thou refuse to let them go. The witnesses note and wilt hold them still.

The witnesses note God conditioning the plague on Pharaoh's own refusal — "if thou refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still" — the judgment no arbitrary stroke but the just answer to his willful obstinacy, in his own power to avert; sinners bring God's judgments on themselves.

So the witnesses make the verse the just ground of the coming judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is that the plague was conditioned on Pharaoh's continued refusal, the judgment his own procuring, in his own power to avert by obeying. The witnesses note the if thou refuse: "if thou refuse to let them go" — Keil notes the conditioned threat; the if thou refuse; the the conditioned threat; the on refusal; the his own; the avert. The expositors observe the not arbitrary: the judgment not arbitrary; the not arbitrary; the not arbitrary; the the just answer; the his disobedience; the in his power. The witnesses press the hold them still: "and wilt hold them still" — active resistance; the hold them still; the active resistance; the keep holding; the in bondage; the settled. The expositors draw the lesson on the laid plainly: the choice laid plainly before him; the laid plainly; the the choice; the plainly; the the blame; the his own. The witnesses observe the his own procuring: the plague of his own procuring; the his own procuring; the his own; the procuring; the seen to be; the brought on. The expositors note the on themselves: sinners bring judgments on themselves; the on themselves; the sinners; the bring; the judgments; the obstinate refusal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the For if thou refuse to let them go, the and wilt hold them still, and the condition. The verse thus records the just ground of the judgment — teaching that the plague was conditioned on Pharaoh's continued refusal, his own procuring.
3:
"Behold, the hand of the LORD is upon thy cattle which is in the field, upon the horses, upon the asses, upon the camels, upon the oxen, and upon the sheep: there shall be a very grievous murrain." The witnesses note the hand of the LORD upon Egypt's cattle, a grievous murrain. Adam Clarke notes "the hand of the LORD" is the power of God manifested in judgment. Gill notes this hand was stronger than Pharaoh's, with which he held the Israelites. The witnesses note God declaring his hand upon the cattle of Egypt — horses, asses, camels, oxen, and sheep — with a very grievous murrain; the fifth plague, God's mighty hand smiting the wealth of Egypt — that God stretches his hand against the beasts: "the hand of the LORD is upon thy cattle which is in the field... there shall be a very grievous murrain"; the witnesses note the figure, "the hand of the LORD," the "power of God manifested in judgment," and (as Gill marks) "stronger than" Pharaoh's hand, with which he held Israel, so that God's hand should overpower the hand of the oppressor; the witnesses note the breadth, "upon the horses, upon the asses, upon the camels, upon the oxen, and upon the sheep" — all the kinds of cattle, the riches and labour of Egypt, struck at once; the witnesses note the severity, "a very grievous murrain" (a deadly pestilence on the beasts), a heavy blow on the substance of the land; the witnesses note that this plague, after those on the bodies and comforts of men, now struck their possessions, God showing his dominion over all and judging Egypt's beast-gods; so that the hand of the LORD smites Egypt's cattle; there shall be a very grievous murrain. The witnesses note Behold, the hand of the LORD is upon thy cattle which is in the field. The witnesses note there shall be a very grievous murrain.

The witnesses note the hand of the LORD — the power of God in judgment, stronger than Pharaoh's oppressing hand — smiting all the cattle of Egypt with a grievous murrain; striking the wealth and labour of the land, and judging Egypt's beast-gods.

So the witnesses make the verse the fifth plague, God's hand on Egypt's cattle. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's mighty hand, stronger than Pharaoh's, smote all the cattle of Egypt with a grievous murrain, striking the wealth of the land. The witnesses note the hand of the LORD: "the hand of the LORD is upon thy cattle" — Clarke notes power in judgment; the hand of the LORD; the Clarke; the power in judgment; the stronger; the than Pharaoh's. The expositors observe the the kinds: "horses... asses... camels... oxen... sheep" — all the cattle; the the kinds; the all the cattle; the horses; the oxen; the sheep. The witnesses press the the wealth: the riches and labour of Egypt; the the wealth; the the riches; the the labour; the of Egypt; the struck. The expositors draw the lesson on the grievous murrain: "a very grievous murrain" — a deadly pestilence; the grievous murrain; the deadly; the pestilence; the the beasts; the heavy. The witnesses observe the their possessions: now struck their possessions; the their possessions; the possessions; the their substance; the after their comforts; the struck. The expositors note the beast-gods: judging Egypt's beast-gods; the beast-gods; the the beast-gods; the idolatry; the judged; the dominion. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Behold the hand of the LORD is upon thy cattle which is in the field, the there shall be a very grievous murrain, and the plague. The verse thus records the fifth plague — teaching that God's mighty hand smote all the cattle of Egypt with a grievous murrain.
4:
"And the LORD shall sever between the cattle of Israel and the cattle of Egypt: and there shall nothing die of all that is the children's of Israel." The witnesses note God severing between the cattle of Israel and Egypt, so that none of Israel's should die. Gill notes the LORD would make such a difference and distinction between them that the murrain should not be on the one as on the other. The witnesses note God promising to sever between the cattle of Israel and Egypt, so that nothing of Israel's should die; the discriminating providence of God, marking off his people from the judgment — that God draws a line between his own and the world: "the LORD shall sever between the cattle of Israel and the cattle of Egypt: and there shall nothing die of all that is the children's of Israel"; the witnesses note the distinction, "the LORD shall sever" — that God would "make such a difference" between the two, that the murrain should fall on Egypt's cattle and not touch Israel's, a thing plainly beyond nature and showing the hand of God; the witnesses note the completeness of the mercy, "there shall nothing die of all that is the children's of Israel" — not one beast of Israel's lost, a perfect preservation; the witnesses note the comfort and the proof in it, that the plague was no chance pestilence but God's own act, since it so exactly spared his people and smote his enemies; the witnesses note the great truth, that God knows how to distinguish between the righteous and the wicked, sparing his own in the day of judgment; so that God severs his people from the plague; nothing die of all that is the children's of Israel. The witnesses note the LORD shall sever between the cattle of Israel and the cattle of Egypt. The witnesses note there shall nothing die of all that is the children's of Israel.

The witnesses note God severing between the cattle of Israel and Egypt — not one of Israel's to die — a distinction plainly beyond nature, proving the plague God's own act; God knows how to distinguish between the righteous and the wicked, sparing his own in the day of judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the discriminating providence of God. The lesson the expositors draw is that God severed between Israel's cattle and Egypt's, sparing his own perfectly, a distinction beyond nature that proved the plague his act. The witnesses note the the LORD shall sever: "the LORD shall sever" — Gill notes a difference made; the the LORD shall sever; the a difference; the made; the distinction; the marked off. The expositors observe the beyond nature: a thing beyond nature; the beyond nature; the beyond; the nature; the the hand of God; the plainly. The witnesses press the nothing die: "nothing die of all that is the children's" — perfect preservation; the nothing die; the perfect; the preservation; the not one; the spared. The expositors draw the lesson on the no chance: no chance pestilence but God's act; the no chance; the no chance; the God's act; the so exactly; the spared. The witnesses observe the distinguish: God distinguishes righteous and wicked; the distinguish; the distinguishes; the the righteous; the the wicked; the sparing. The expositors note the his own spared: his own spared in judgment; the his own spared; the his own; the spared; the the day; the judgment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the LORD shall sever between the cattle of Israel and the cattle of Egypt, the there shall nothing die of all that is the children's of Israel, and the distinction. The verse thus records the discriminating providence — teaching that God severed his people from the plague, sparing his own perfectly.
5:
"And the LORD appointed a set time, saying, To morrow the LORD shall do this thing in the land." The witnesses note God appointing a set time for the plague, to come on the morrow. Adam Clarke notes "To-morrow" the LORD shall do this thing, a set and definite time named. Gill notes God appointed a set time for the coming of this plague, that it might plainly appear it came from him and was not owing to any natural cause. The witnesses note God appointing a set time, that on the morrow he would do this thing in the land; the fixing of the hour beforehand, the proof that the plague was God's own act — that God names the very time: "the LORD appointed a set time, saying, To morrow the LORD shall do this thing in the land"; the witnesses note the appointing of "a set time," that God fixed the exact hour ("to morrow") when the murrain should fall; the witnesses note the reason Gill marks, that God set the time beforehand "that it might plainly appear it came from him, and was not owing to any natural cause" — for no natural pestilence comes at a named hour, but this should, proving it the act of God; the witnesses note the certainty, that what God foretold to the day he would surely perform, his word and his work agreeing exactly; the witnesses note that God's giving Pharaoh until "to morrow" was also a space to repent, a last warning before the blow; the witnesses note the sovereignty of God, who orders the times of his judgments; so that God fixes the very hour of the plague; To morrow the LORD shall do this thing in the land. The witnesses note And the LORD appointed a set time. The witnesses note To morrow the LORD shall do this thing in the land.

The witnesses note God appointing a set time — "to morrow" — that the plague might plainly appear his act and no natural pestilence, which comes at no named hour; the certainty that what God foretold to the day he would surely perform, with a space to repent.

So the witnesses make the verse the fixing of the hour, the proof of God's act. The lesson the expositors draw is that God named the time beforehand, that the plague might appear his own act and not a natural cause, his word and work agreeing exactly. The witnesses note the set time: "the LORD appointed a set time" — Gill notes that it appear from him; the set time; the from him; the appointed; the the hour; the fixed. The expositors observe the to morrow: "To morrow the LORD shall do this thing" — the exact time; the to morrow; the the exact time; the the morrow; the named; the definite. The witnesses press the not natural: not a natural cause; the not natural; the not natural; the no pestilence; the at a named hour; the God's act. The expositors draw the lesson on the the certainty: what God foretold he performs; the the certainty; the foretold; the performs; the word and work; the agree. The witnesses observe the a space: a space to repent; the a space; the to repent; the until morrow; the a warning; the before the blow. The expositors note the sovereignty: God orders the times; the sovereignty; the God; the orders; the the times; the his judgments. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And the LORD appointed a set time, the To morrow the LORD shall do this thing in the land, and the set time. The verse thus records the fixing of the hour — teaching that God named the time beforehand, that the plague might appear his own act and not a natural cause.
6:
"And the LORD did that thing on the morrow, and all the cattle of Egypt died: but of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one." The witnesses note the plague falling exactly as foretold, all Egypt's cattle dying but none of Israel's. Gill notes the LORD brought a murrain, a pestilential disease, on the cattle on the morrow, according to the set time. The witnesses note the LORD doing the thing on the morrow, so that all the cattle of Egypt died, but not one of Israel's; the exact fulfilment of the word and the perfect distinction kept — that God performs precisely what he said: "the LORD did that thing on the morrow, and all the cattle of Egypt died: but of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one"; the witnesses note the punctual fulfilment, "the LORD did that thing on the morrow," at the very time appointed, the murrain falling to the day; the witnesses note the greatness of the stroke, "all the cattle of Egypt died" — that is, the cattle in the field (v.3), a vast destruction of Egypt's wealth and substance; the witnesses note the kept distinction, "but of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one" — the promise (v.4) fulfilled to the letter, not a single beast of Israel's lost, a manifest miracle of discrimination; the witnesses note that the exact agreement of the event with the word, and the sparing of Israel, proved beyond doubt that this was the hand of God; the witnesses note that God is faithful both in his threatenings and in his promises; so that the plague falls exactly, Egypt's cattle dying, Israel's spared; of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one. The witnesses note the LORD did that thing on the morrow, and all the cattle of Egypt died. The witnesses note but of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one.

The witnesses note the plague falling exactly as foretold, to the day — all Egypt's cattle in the field dying, but not one of Israel's — the promise kept to the letter; the exact agreement of event with word, and the sparing of Israel, proving beyond doubt the hand of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the exact fulfilment and the kept distinction. The lesson the expositors draw is that God performed precisely what he said, all Egypt's cattle dying and none of Israel's, the word fulfilled to the letter. The witnesses note the on the morrow: "the LORD did that thing on the morrow" — Gill notes the set time; the on the morrow; the the set time; the punctual; the to the day; the appointed. The expositors observe the all Egypt's cattle: "all the cattle of Egypt died" — in the field; the all Egypt's cattle; the in the field; the a vast destruction; the the wealth; the the substance. The witnesses press the died not one: "of Israel... died not one" — the promise kept; the died not one; the the promise; the kept; the to the letter; the not one beast. The expositors draw the lesson on the the distinction: the kept distinction; the the distinction; the kept; the the miracle; the discrimination; the manifest. The witnesses observe the the hand of God: proved the hand of God; the the hand of God; the beyond doubt. The expositors note the faithful: God faithful in threat and promise; the faithful; the in threat; the in promise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one, and the fulfilment. The verse thus records the exact fulfilment — teaching that God performed precisely what he said, all Egypt's cattle dying and none of Israel's.
7:
"And Pharaoh sent, and, behold, there was not one of the cattle of the Israelites dead. And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people go." The witnesses note Pharaoh sending to verify the distinction, yet hardening his heart still. Gill notes Pharaoh sent messengers to the land of Goshen to see whether the murrain was upon the cattle of Israel or not, and whether any died. The witnesses note Pharaoh sending and finding not one of Israel's cattle dead, yet his heart being hardened so that he would not let the people go; the proof rejected, Pharaoh hardening against the plainest evidence — that even the verified miracle does not move him: "Pharaoh sent, and, behold, there was not one of the cattle of the Israelites dead. And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened"; the witnesses note the inquiry, that Pharaoh "sent" to Goshen to learn whether Israel's cattle were spared, and found the report true, "not one... dead" — a fact he himself proved; the witnesses note the dreadful hardening, "the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people go" — that, even having proved the distinction and so the hand of God, he still would not yield, his obstinacy growing against the clearest light; the witnesses note that he had every means to be convinced, and was, in fact, convinced of the truth, yet hardened himself against it; the witnesses note the dreadful state of a heart that will not bend even to evidence it cannot deny; so that Pharaoh, proving the miracle, yet hardens and refuses; he did not let the people go. The witnesses note And Pharaoh sent, and, behold, there was not one of the cattle of the Israelites dead. The witnesses note And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people go.

The witnesses note Pharaoh himself sending to verify the distinction, finding not one of Israel's cattle dead, yet hardening his heart still — hardening against the plainest evidence he himself had proved; the dreadful state of a heart that will not bend even to truth it cannot deny.

So the witnesses make the verse the proof rejected and the heart hardened. The lesson the expositors draw is that Pharaoh verified the sparing of Israel, and so the hand of God, yet hardened himself against the clearest light and would not let the people go. The witnesses note the Pharaoh sent: "Pharaoh sent" — Gill notes to Goshen to learn; the Pharaoh sent; the to Goshen; the to learn; the the cattle; the spared. The expositors observe the not one dead: "not one... dead" — a fact he proved; the not one dead; the a fact; the proved; the the report; the true. The witnesses press the heart hardened: "the heart of Pharaoh was hardened" — against the evidence; the heart hardened; the against the evidence; the obstinacy; the growing; the the light. The expositors draw the lesson on the would not yield: "he did not let the people go" — still refused; the would not yield; the would not; the refused; the the people; the go. The witnesses observe the convinced yet hardened: convinced yet hardened himself; the convinced yet hardened; the convinced; the yet hardened; the the truth; the against. The expositors note the will not bend: a heart that will not bend; the will not bend; the not bend; the to evidence; the cannot deny; the dreadful. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And Pharaoh sent and behold there was not one of the cattle of the Israelites dead, the And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened and he did not let the people go, and the proof rejected. The verse thus records the proof rejected — teaching that Pharaoh verified the distinction yet hardened himself against the clearest light.
8:
"And the LORD said unto Moses and unto Aaron, Take to you handfuls of ashes of the furnace, and let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh." The witnesses note God bidding Moses take ashes of the furnace and sprinkle them toward heaven before Pharaoh. Keil and Delitzsch note God said, Take handfuls of ashes of the furnace, and let Moses sprinkle it toward heaven in the sight of Pharaoh. The witnesses note God commanding Moses and Aaron to take handfuls of furnace-ashes and Moses to sprinkle them toward heaven in Pharaoh's sight; the sign of the sixth plague, the ashes cast up to bring the boils — that God appoints a new and striking means: "Take to you handfuls of ashes of the furnace, and let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh"; the witnesses note the means, "handfuls of ashes of the furnace" — a small and common thing, taken perhaps from a brick-kiln (the very furnaces of Israel's bondage), to be made the instrument of a great plague; the witnesses note the manner, "let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh" — that Moses, openly before the king, should cast the ashes up into the air; the witnesses note the fitness some find, that the ashes of the furnace, where Israel had toiled, should turn to a plague on their oppressors, a measure of recompense; the witnesses note that God can make the least things the instruments of his judgments, that the power may be seen to be his; so that God appoints the ashes for the sixth plague; let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh. The witnesses note Take to you handfuls of ashes of the furnace. The witnesses note and let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh.

The witnesses note God appointing handfuls of furnace-ashes — a small and common thing, perhaps from the brick-kilns of Israel's bondage — to be cast up before Pharaoh as the means of the boils; God making the least things the instruments of his judgments, that the power be seen to be his.

So the witnesses make the verse the sign of the sixth plague. The lesson the expositors draw is that God bade Moses cast up furnace-ashes before Pharaoh as the means of the boils, the least things made instruments of judgment. The witnesses note the ashes of the furnace: "handfuls of ashes of the furnace" — Gill notes a common thing; the ashes of the furnace; the a common thing; the small; the the brick-kiln; the the bondage. The expositors observe the sprinkle toward heaven: "sprinkle it toward the heaven" — cast up openly; the sprinkle toward heaven; the cast up; the openly; the the air; the before Pharaoh. The witnesses press the in Pharaoh's sight: "in the sight of Pharaoh" — before the king; the in Pharaoh's sight; the before the king; the openly; the the court; the seen. The expositors draw the lesson on the the recompense: the ashes of bondage a recompense; the the recompense; the the ashes; the of bondage; the their toil; the on the oppressor. The witnesses observe the least things: God uses the least things; the least things; the least; the things; the instruments; the judgments. The expositors note the power be his: that the power be his; the power be his; the the power; the his; the seen; the the means. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Take to you handfuls of ashes of the furnace, the and let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh, and the sign. The verse thus records the sign of the sixth plague — teaching that God bade Moses cast up furnace-ashes as the means of the boils.
9:
"And it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt, and shall be a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast, throughout all the land of Egypt." The witnesses note the ashes becoming dust over all Egypt, bringing boils on man and beast. Gill notes the ashes, thrown into the air, should be so multiplied and spread as to be over all the land of Egypt, coming down everywhere of a hot and scalding nature. The witnesses note God foretelling that the ashes should become small dust over all Egypt, and be a boil breaking forth with blains on man and beast; the sixth plague, the painful sores on every living body — that the cast-up ashes should spread and fall as a plague: "it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt, and shall be a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast"; the witnesses note the spreading, "it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt" — that the few handfuls, cast up, should be so multiplied and scattered by God as to cover the whole land, falling everywhere "of a hot and scalding nature"; the witnesses note the effect, "a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast" — painful inflamed sores, ulcers breaking out with pustules, on the bodies of men and the remaining beasts alike; the witnesses note the universality, "throughout all the land of Egypt" — that none escaped, the plague reaching every person and creature; the witnesses note that this plague, after the loss of cattle, now touched the bodies of the Egyptians themselves, a nearer and sorer affliction; so that the dust brings boils on all Egypt; a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast. The witnesses note it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt. The witnesses note and shall be a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast.

The witnesses note the few handfuls of ash, cast up, multiplied by God to cover all Egypt and fall as painful boils on man and beast alike — the plague now touching the bodies of the Egyptians themselves, a nearer and sorer affliction, none escaping.

So the witnesses make the verse the spreading dust and the painful boils. The lesson the expositors draw is that the cast-up ashes covered all Egypt and brought boils on every man and beast, the plague now touching their own bodies. The witnesses note the small dust: "it shall become small dust" — Gill notes multiplied and spread; the small dust; the multiplied; the spread; the all the land; the scattered. The expositors observe the hot and scalding: of a hot and scalding nature; the hot and scalding; the hot; the scalding; the falling; the everywhere. The witnesses press the boil with blains: "a boil breaking forth with blains" — painful sores; the boil with blains; the painful; the sores; the ulcers; the pustules. The expositors draw the lesson on the man and beast: "upon man, and upon beast" — on every body; the man and beast; the man; the beast; the every body; the alike. The witnesses observe the throughout the land: "throughout all the land" — none escaped; the throughout the land; the all the land; the none escaped; the every person; the every creature. The expositors note the their own bodies: now touched their own bodies; the their own bodies; the nearer; the sorer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and shall be a boil breaking forth with blains upon man and upon beast, and the boils. The verse thus records the spreading dust and the boils — teaching that the ashes covered all Egypt and brought painful sores on every man and beast.
10:
"And they took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh; and Moses sprinkled it up toward heaven; and it became a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast." The witnesses note the sign performed, the ashes sprinkled and the boils breaking forth. Charles Ellicott notes Moses sprinkled it up toward heaven, presenting it, as it were, to God, in evidence of his people's wrongs; the "boil breaking forth with blains" being an inflammation producing pustules. The witnesses note Moses and Aaron taking the ashes, standing before Pharaoh, Moses sprinkling them toward heaven, and the boils breaking forth on man and beast; the open execution of the sixth plague, exactly as appointed — that the word is fulfilled before the king: "they took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh; and Moses sprinkled it up toward heaven; and it became a boil breaking forth with blains"; the witnesses note the obedience, that they "took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh," doing openly as God commanded; the witnesses note the meaning Ellicott draws from the gesture, that Moses "sprinkled it up toward heaven, presenting it, as it were, to God, in evidence of his people's wrongs," appealing to heaven against the oppressor; the witnesses note the immediate effect, "it became a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast" — the inflamed, ulcerous sores at once breaking out on all, the plague following instantly on the sign; the witnesses note that God performed exactly and at once what he had threatened, his word and work agreeing; so that the boils break forth on all Egypt before Pharaoh; it became a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast. The witnesses note they took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh; and Moses sprinkled it up toward heaven. The witnesses note and it became a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast.

The witnesses note the sign performed openly before Pharaoh — the ashes sprinkled toward heaven (as if presenting them to God in evidence of his people's wrongs) and the boils at once breaking forth on man and beast; God performing exactly and instantly what he had threatened.

So the witnesses make the verse the open execution of the sixth plague. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses cast up the ashes before Pharaoh and the boils at once broke forth, God performing exactly and instantly what he threatened. The witnesses note the took the ashes: "they took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh" — obeying openly; the took the ashes; the obeying; the openly; the the ashes; the before Pharaoh. 11:
"And the magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils; for the boil was upon the magicians, and upon all the Egyptians." The witnesses note the magicians unable to stand before Moses, themselves smitten with the boils. Gill notes the boils were upon them as on others, which with all their art they could not keep off; they were so sore and painful that they were obliged to withdraw and could not stand their ground. The witnesses note the magicians being unable to stand before Moses because of the boils, which were upon them and all the Egyptians; the final defeat and shame of the magicians, smitten by the very plague — that the sorcerers who had withstood Moses are themselves struck down: "the magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils; for the boil was upon the magicians, and upon all the Egyptians"; the witnesses note the utter overthrow of the magicians, that they "could not stand before Moses" — not now even to imitate, but driven from his presence, "obliged to withdraw," their art helpless to ward off the plague from their own bodies; the witnesses note the special note, "the boil was upon the magicians" — that they who had contested with God's servant were themselves smitten, covered with the painful sores like all the rest, their pretended power exposed as nothing; the witnesses note the justice and the shame, that the leaders of Egypt's resistance were now the open trophies of God's victory, unable so much as to stand; the witnesses note that God brings to shame all the powers that set themselves against him; so that the magicians are smitten and driven away in defeat; for the boil was upon the magicians, and upon all the Egyptians. 12:
"And the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had spoken unto Moses." The witnesses note that here the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, after his long self-hardening. Gill notes that, Pharaoh having often and so long hardened his own heart, God now gave him up to judicial hardness of heart, to his own corruptions, the temptations of Satan, and the lying magicians about him. The witnesses note the LORD hardening the heart of Pharaoh, so that he hearkened not, as God had spoken; the first plain word that God hardened Pharaoh, a judicial giving-up after long self-hardening — that, the man having hardened himself again and again, God now hardens him: "the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had spoken"; the witnesses note the solemn note the commentators mark, that here for the first time it is said plainly "the LORD hardened" Pharaoh, where before he had hardened "his own heart" (7:13, 8:15, 32); the witnesses note the careful sense in which this judicial hardening is understood — that, "Pharaoh having often, and so long, hardened his own heart, God gave him up to judicial hardness," not by infusing any sin, but by withdrawing restraining grace and leaving him to "his own corruptions, the temptations of Satan, and the lying magicians," so that he made an ill use of all; the witnesses note that this is the righteous judgment of God, who gives men up to the hardness they have chosen, yet without himself being the author of their sin; the witnesses note the fulfilment, "as the LORD had spoken unto Moses," that the foretold hardening (4:21, 7:3) came to pass; so that God judicially hardens the heart Pharaoh first hardened; as the LORD had spoken unto Moses. The witnesses note And the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he hearkened not unto them. The witnesses note as the LORD had spoken unto Moses.

The witnesses note the first plain word that "the LORD hardened" Pharaoh — a judicial giving-up after his long self-hardening; God not infusing sin but withdrawing restraining grace and leaving him to his own corruptions; the righteous judgment of God, who gives men up to the hardness they have chosen.

So the witnesses make the verse the judicial hardening of Pharaoh. The lesson the expositors draw is that, after Pharaoh's long self-hardening, God judicially gave him up to the hardness he had chosen, not as the author of his sin but by withdrawing grace. 13:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh, and say unto him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me." The witnesses note God sending Moses early to stand before Pharaoh with the renewed demand. Matthew Henry notes here is a general declaration of the wrath of God against Pharaoh for his obstinacy. The witnesses note God bidding Moses rise early, stand before Pharaoh, and again demand in the name of the LORD God of the Hebrews that he let the people go; the renewed and solemn demand opening the seventh plague — that God presses the same word with new weight: "Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh... Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me"; the witnesses note the charge, "Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh" — that Moses should meet the king betimes (when, it seems, he went out early), losing no time in pressing God's claim; the witnesses note the same authority and demand, "Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me" — the unchanging requirement, now backed by the threat of the heaviest plagues; the witnesses note that this opening is followed by "a general declaration of the wrath of God" against Pharaoh's obstinacy, the most solemn of the warnings; the witnesses note the patience of God yet again, warning before the sorest stroke; so that God renews the demand before the great plague of hail; Let my people go, that they may serve me. The witnesses note Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh. The witnesses note Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me.

14:
"For I will at this time send all my plagues upon thine heart, and upon thy servants, and upon thy people; that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth." The witnesses note God threatening to send all his plagues, that Pharaoh might know there is none like him. Gill notes this means rather all the plagues yet to come, especially as the hail was the most terrible to Pharaoh; the design being that he might know none is like God in all the earth. The witnesses note God declaring he would send all his plagues upon Pharaoh's heart, his servants, and his people, that he might know there is none like God in all the earth; the threat of the full weight of judgment, with its great design — that God warns of heavier strokes to come: "I will at this time send all my plagues upon thine heart... that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth"; the witnesses note the threat, "all my plagues upon thine heart" — that the remaining and sorer plagues should now come, reaching even to Pharaoh's "heart," the seat of his pride, to break his obstinacy; the witnesses note the breadth, "and upon thy servants, and upon thy people" — that the whole nation should share the judgment with the king; the witnesses note the great design, "that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth" — that the plagues were sent to teach Pharaoh and Egypt the incomparable greatness of God, above all the gods they worshipped, there being "none like" him; the witnesses note that God's judgments are meant to make men know his supremacy; so that God threatens the full weight of plagues, to show his supremacy; that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth. So the witnesses make the verse the threat of the full judgment and its design. The lesson the expositors draw is that God would send all his plagues, reaching to Pharaoh's heart and his people, that they might know there is none like God in all the earth. 15:
"For now I will stretch out my hand, that I may smite thee and thy people with pestilence; and thou shalt be cut off from the earth." The witnesses note God's word that he could have smitten Pharaoh with pestilence and cut him off. Gill notes these words are rather to be rendered, not in the future, but that God might have stretched out his hand and smitten Pharaoh with pestilence, cutting him off; yet he had not done so. The witnesses note God saying he could have stretched out his hand and smitten Pharaoh and his people with pestilence, cutting them off from the earth; the declaration of God's power to have destroyed Pharaoh long since — that God shows what he might justly have done: "For now I will stretch out my hand, that I may smite thee and thy people with pestilence; and thou shalt be cut off from the earth"; the witnesses note the better rendering the commentators give, that the sense is not that God now would do this, but that he might have done it — "I might have stretched out my hand... and thou shouldest have been cut off" — for God had not in fact destroyed Pharaoh by pestilence; the witnesses note the point of it, that God could justly have smitten Pharaoh and his people with a deadly plague and "cut" them "off from the earth" long before, blotting them out at a stroke; the witnesses note that this sets up the wonder of the next verse, that God had not done so but had spared and preserved Pharaoh for a further purpose; the witnesses note the truth of God's sovereign power and right over the life of the proudest king; so that God declares he might have cut Pharaoh off, but had not; and thou shalt be cut off from the earth. The witnesses note For now I will stretch out my hand, that I may smite thee and thy people with pestilence. The witnesses note and thou shalt be cut off from the earth.

The witnesses note God's word — better rendered that he might have stretched out his hand and cut Pharaoh off by pestilence long since — declaring what he could justly have done; setting up the wonder of the next verse, that God had not done so but spared Pharaoh for a further purpose.

So the witnesses make the verse God's power to have destroyed Pharaoh. The lesson the expositors draw is that God might justly have cut Pharaoh off by pestilence long since, yet had not, sparing him for a further purpose. The witnesses note the stretch out my hand: "I will stretch out my hand" — Gill notes might have done it; the stretch out my hand; the might have; the done it; the the rendering; the could. 16:
"And in very deed for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power; and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth." The witnesses note the great declaration of why God raised up and preserved Pharaoh. Gill notes the sense is, instead of cutting thee off, "I have raised thee up," made thee to stand and continue in being, preserved thee from perishing by the former plagues, and reserved thee for greater judgments, that my power and name might be shown. The witnesses note God declaring that for this cause he had raised Pharaoh up, to show in him his power and that his name might be declared throughout all the earth; the supreme statement of God's sovereign purpose in Pharaoh — that God reveals the end of his sparing and using the proud king: "for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power; and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth"; the witnesses note the sense of "raised thee up," that, instead of cutting Pharaoh off, God had "made thee to stand, to continue in being," preserved him through the plagues and reserved him as the stage on which to display his power; the witnesses note the twofold purpose, "to shew in thee my power" (that God's mighty hand might be seen in the judgments wrought on so great and stubborn a king) and "that my name may be declared throughout all the earth" (that the fame of God's power should spread to all nations and ages); the witnesses note the great use the Apostle Paul makes of this verse (Rom. 9:17), proving God's sovereign right to deal with men as he will, to show his power and glory; the witnesses note that God overrules even the rebellion of the wicked to the spreading of his own glory; so that God raised Pharaoh up to show his power and declare his name; that my name may be declared throughout all the earth. The witnesses note for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power. The witnesses note and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth.

The witnesses note the supreme statement of God's purpose — "for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power; and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth" — that God preserved the proud king as the stage to display his power; the very verse Paul cites (Romans 9:17) to prove God's sovereign right.

So the witnesses make the verse the supreme statement of God's sovereign purpose in Pharaoh. The lesson the expositors draw is that God raised up and preserved Pharaoh to show his power in him and spread his name through all the earth, the verse Paul cites for God's sovereignty. The witnesses note the raised thee up: "for this cause have I raised thee up" — Gill notes made thee to stand; the raised thee up; the preserved; the reserved. The expositors observe the to shew my power: "to shew in thee my power" — the mighty hand seen; the to shew my power; the the mighty hand. The witnesses press the my name declared: "my name may be declared throughout all the earth" — the fame spread; the my name declared; the all the earth. The expositors draw the lesson on the Paul cites it: Paul cites it for God's sovereignty; the Paul cites it; the Paul; the Romans 9:17; the sovereignty; the his right. The expositors note the the stage: Pharaoh the stage of God's power; the the stage; the display. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth, and the great declaration. The verse thus records God's sovereign purpose in Pharaoh — teaching that God raised him up to show his power and spread his name through all the earth.
17:
"As yet exaltest thou thyself against my people, that thou wilt not let them go?" The witnesses note God charging Pharaoh with exalting himself against his people. Gill notes Pharaoh exalted himself against God's people, and so against God himself, disobeying his commands, despising his messengers, and slighting his miracles, showing intolerable pride and insolence. The witnesses note God charging Pharaoh with still exalting himself against his people, refusing to let them go; the rebuke of Pharaoh's proud and insolent obstinacy — that God lays bare the root of Pharaoh's sin: "As yet exaltest thou thyself against my people, that thou wilt not let them go?"; the witnesses note the charge of pride, "exaltest thou thyself against my people" — that Pharaoh, in keeping Israel and resisting their release, lifted himself up against God's own people, and so against God himself, in "intolerable pride and insolence"; the witnesses note the "as yet," that, after so many plagues and proofs, Pharaoh still persisted, his pride unbroken, exalting himself against the God who had so plainly shown his power; the witnesses note the heart of his sin, that it was not weakness but pride, a willful exalting of himself against the Almighty, "disobeying his commands, despising his messengers, and slighting his miracles"; the witnesses note that pride against God and his people is a great and provoking sin, drawing down the sorest judgments; so that God rebukes Pharaoh's proud self-exalting; that thou wilt not let them go? The witnesses note As yet exaltest thou thyself against my people. The witnesses note that thou wilt not let them go?

The witnesses note God charging Pharaoh with exalting himself against his people — and so against God himself — in intolerable pride; that, after so many plagues, he still persisted, his pride unbroken; pride against God and his people a great and provoking sin, drawing down the sorest judgments.

So the witnesses make the verse the rebuke of Pharaoh's proud obstinacy. The lesson the expositors draw is that Pharaoh, in resisting Israel's release, exalted himself against God and his people in willful pride, the root of his sin laid bare. The witnesses note the exaltest thyself: "exaltest thou thyself against my people" — Gill notes intolerable pride; the exaltest thyself; the intolerable pride; the against my people; the and against God; the insolence. The expositors observe the as yet: "As yet" — still persisting; the as yet; the still; the persisting; the after the plagues; the unbroken. The witnesses press the not weakness but pride: not weakness but pride; the not weakness but pride; the not weakness; the pride; the willful; the the heart. The expositors draw the lesson on the despising God: despising God's commands and messengers; the despising God; the despising; the his commands; the his messengers; the his miracles. The witnesses observe the will not let go: "that thou wilt not let them go" — refusing still; the will not let go; the refusing; the still; the the people; the keep. The expositors note the a provoking sin: pride a great provoking sin; the a provoking sin; the pride; the provoking; the great; the the judgments. The verse thus records the rebuke of Pharaoh's pride — teaching that he exalted himself against God and his people in willful pride, the root of his sin.
18:
"Behold, to morrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail, such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now." The witnesses note God announcing a hail more grievous than any since Egypt's foundation. Keil and Delitzsch note God said, To morrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail, such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now. The witnesses note God foretelling that on the morrow he would rain a very grievous hail, such as had never been in Egypt; the announcement of the seventh plague, unmatched in all Egypt's history — that God names the time and the unequalled greatness of the hail: "to morrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail, such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now"; the witnesses note the set time again, "to morrow about this time," that God fixed the very hour, a space for Pharaoh to repent and a proof it should be God's act; the witnesses note the greatness, "a very grievous hail" — a storm of hail, with thunder and lightning and rain, the most terrible of the plagues, that (as Aben Ezra noted) more terrified Pharaoh than any other; the witnesses note the unequalled measure, "such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now" — a hail beyond any in all the long history of the land, which (lying in a rainless region) scarcely knew hail at all; the witnesses note that God can arm the very heavens against his enemies, the elements his soldiers; so that God announces a hail past all example in Egypt; such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now. The witnesses note Behold, to morrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail. The witnesses note such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now.

The witnesses note God announcing a hail more grievous than any since Egypt's foundation — the most terrible of the plagues, in a land that scarcely knew hail at all; God fixing the hour as a space to repent and a proof of his act, arming the very heavens against his enemies.

So the witnesses make the verse the announcement of the unequalled hail. The lesson the expositors draw is that God foretold a hail past all example in Egypt's history, the most terrible of the plagues, the heavens armed against his enemies. The witnesses note the to morrow: "to morrow about this time" — Gill notes the set time; the to morrow; the the set time; the the hour; the a space; the to repent. The expositors observe the grievous hail: "a very grievous hail" — the most terrible; the grievous hail; the the most terrible; the the storm; the thunder; the lightning. The witnesses press the terrified Pharaoh: more terrified than any other; the terrified Pharaoh; the terrified; the more than any; the Aben Ezra; the the worst. The expositors draw the lesson on the not been since: "such as hath not been... since the foundation" — unequalled; the not been since; the unequalled; the the foundation; the all the history; the never. The expositors note the heavens armed: God arms the heavens; the heavens armed; the the elements. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now, and the announcement. The verse thus records the announcement of the unequalled hail — teaching that God foretold a hail past all example, the heavens armed against his enemies.
